eli3: Entropy measures uncertainty or surprise in a random variable. A fair coin has 1 bit of entropy (max uncertainty). A biased coin has less — you're less surprised by the outcome. A coin that always lands heads has zero entropy (no uncertainty).

Formula: H(X) = -Σ p(x) log p(x). High entropy = unpredictable (uniform distribution). Low entropy = predictable (concentrated distribution). Entropy bounds how much you can compress data without loss.
Shannon showed that the minimum average number of bits needed to encode outcomes from distribution p is H(p). This is the fundamental limit of lossless compression.

whenWhy.title: Data compression
whenWhy.body: Huffman coding and arithmetic coding approach entropy limit. Know the distribution, assign shorter codes to frequent symbols. Entropy gives theoretical minimum bits per symbol. Used in ZIP, JPEG, video codecs.

whenWhy.title: Decision trees and feature selection
whenWhy.body: Information gain = entropy(parent) - weighted sum entropy(children). Measures how much a feature reduces uncertainty. ID3, C4.5, CART use information gain (or Gini impurity) to choose splits.

whenWhy.title: Policy entropy in reinforcement learning
whenWhy.body: Adding entropy bonus to RL objectives encourages exploration. Prevents premature convergence to deterministic policies. SAC (Soft Actor-Critic) maximizes expected return plus policy entropy.

whenWhy.title: Model uncertainty and Bayesian inference
whenWhy.body: Entropy of posterior measures uncertainty. Maximum entropy principle: choose distribution with maximum entropy subject to constraints. Used for prior specification and density estimation.

deepDiveIntro: For discrete random variable X with PMF p(x), Shannon entropy is H(X) = -Σ_x p(x) log p(x) = E[-log p(X)]. Usually base-2 logarithm (bits), sometimes natural log (nats). H(X) ≥ 0 with equality iff X is deterministic. Maximum entropy for finite support is log|X| achieved by uniform distribution.

Joint entropy H(X,Y) = -Σ p(x,y) log p(x,y). Conditional entropy H(Y|X) = -Σ p(x,y) log p(y|x) = H(X,Y) - H(X). Chain rule: H(X₁,…,Xₙ) = Σᵢ H(Xᵢ|X₁,…,Xᵢ₋₁). Differential entropy extends to continuous variables: h(X) = -∫ f(x) log f(x) dx (but can be negative).

deepDive.heading: Shannon Entropy
deepDive.body: H(X) = -Σ p(x) log p(x). Measures expected surprise (information content). Properties: H(X) ≥ 0; H(X) ≤ log|X| with equality for uniform; H(X) = 0 iff X deterministic. Concave function of p — maximum at uniform.

deepDive.heading: Joint and Conditional Entropy
deepDive.body: Joint: H(X,Y) measures uncertainty in pair. Conditional: H(Y|X) = H(X,Y) - H(X) measures remaining uncertainty in Y given X. H(Y|X) = 0 iff Y is function of X. Chain rule: joint = sum of conditionals.

deepDive.heading: Relative Entropy (KL Divergence)
deepDive.body: D(p||q) = Σ p(x) log(p(x)/q(x)) = E_p[log(p/q)]. Not symmetric, not a metric. Measures extra bits needed to encode from p using code optimized for q. D(p||q) ≥ 0 with equality iff p = q.

deepDive.heading: Mutual Information
deepDive.body: I(X;Y) = H(X) - H(X|Y) = H(Y) - H(Y|X) = H(X) + H(Y) - H(X,Y). Measures reduction in uncertainty about X from knowing Y. Symmetric, non-negative, I(X;Y) = 0 iff X ⟂ Y. I(X;Y) = D(p(x,y)||p(x)p(y)).

deepDive.heading: Data Inequality and Compression
deepDive.body: Source coding theorem: expected length of optimal prefix code satisfies H(X) ≤ L* < H(X) + 1. Asymptotically achievable with block coding. Huffman coding is optimal symbol-by-symbol; arithmetic coding approaches entropy for long blocks.

takeaways: Entropy H(X) = -Σ p(x) log p(x): measures uncertainty/surprise.
Conditional entropy H(Y|X): remaining uncertainty after knowing X.
KL divergence D(p||q): extra cost of using q when true is p.
Mutual information I(X;Y): reduction in uncertainty, symmetric.

edgeCases.a: Convention: 0 log 0 = 0 (continuous extension). Makes sense: outcomes with probability 0 contribute nothing to entropy (never occur, so no surprise). This is why we can sum over support only.

Yes. Unlike discrete entropy, differential entropy h(X) = -∫ f(x) log f(x)dx can be negative. Example: Uniform[0,a] has h = log(a), negative for a < 1. Differential entropy is not invariant under transformations; relative entropy (KL) is better behaved.

edgeCases.q: What's the difference between bits and nats?
edgeCases.a: Bits use log₂; nats use ln (natural log). 1 nat = log₂(e) ≈ 1.443 bits. Information theory traditionally uses bits. In optimization (e.g., cross-entropy loss), natural log is common for mathematical convenience. Convert: H_bits = H_nats / ln(2).

sources.label: Cover & Thomas — Elements of Information Theory (2nd ed.)
MacKay — Information Theory, Inference, and Learning Algorithms
